paper_metadata.title: CTRL: A Conditional Transformer Language Model for Controllable Generation
paper_metadata.abstract: Large-scale language models show promising text generation capabilities, but users cannot easily control particular aspects of the generated text. We release CTRL, a 1.63 billion-parameter conditional transformer language model, trained to condition on control codes that govern style, content, and task-specific behavior. Control codes were derived from structure that naturally co-occurs with raw text, preserving the advantages of unsupervised learning while providing more explicit control over text generation. These codes also allow CTRL to predict which parts of the training data are most likely given a sequence. This provides a potential method for analyzing large amounts of data via model-based source attribution. We have released multiple full-sized, pretrained versions of CTRL at https://github.com/salesforce/ctrl.

extraction_data.innovation: CTRL introduces control codes that govern style, content, and task-specific behavior, enabling explicit control over text generation while preserving the advantages of unsupervised learning. These codes also allow model-based source attribution by predicting training data subsets likely given a sequence.
extraction_data.pretraining_task: Conditional language modeling with control codes
extraction_data.pretraining_corpus: Wikipedia (En, De, Es, Fr), Project Gutenberg, 45 subreddits, OpenWebText, news data (Hermann et al., 2015; Barrault et al., 2019; Sandhaus, 2008; Grusky et al.)
extraction_data.extension: Control codes embedded in the input sequence enable conditional generation based on domain, subdomain, entities, and task-specific templates, extending the model's ability to follow structured constraints during inference.
extraction_data.research_problem: Controllable text generation in large-scale language models
extraction_data.application: Domain-specific text generation, task-specific generation (e.g., question answering, translation), zero-shot code-mixing, and fine-grained control over generated content (e.g., ratings, titles, entities)
